claim: Available public data sources are sufficient to establish that remote sensing can identify desert locust breeding habitats at sub-regional scale (MODIS NDVI, CHIRPS rainfall, SMAP soil moisture) and to model swarm movement trajectories using ERA5 wind fields, with FAO Locust Watch bulletins providing field-reported ground truth. However, no public dataset provides real-time or weekly locust presence/absence data at district-level grain for East Africa, and the transition from habitat detection to actionable swarm formation warning remains unvalidated at the lead-time precision needed for operational use. The available data can support sub-regional breeding habitat suitability analysis and hindcast swarm trajectory validation, but cannot produce a reliable operational early warning signal without supplementary field confirmation data that is not currently publicly available.
failure_modes: A reader treats NDVI green-up as proof of locust breeding rather than a habitat suitability indicator, conflating vegetation presence with actual egg-laying or hopper development.
A reader uses CHIRPS rainfall as a direct trigger for swarm formation when rainfall is necessary but not sufficient; soil temperature, moisture, and vegetation type must also be suitable.
A reader treats FAO Locust Watch bulletins as a complete census of swarm activity when they reflect survey team access, which is severely limited in conflict zones.

A decision-maker uses ERA5 wind trajectory models to justify pesticide pre-positioning without acknowledging that 0.25 degree resolution cannot resolve terrain-channelled wind patterns that control local swarm movement.

A reader treats the 60 percent breeding-site explanation rate from Gituku et al. 2024 as an accuracy figure for a predictive model, when it is an in-sample explanatory rate for a single upsurge period.

A reader generalizes the cyclone-breeding linkage from Salih et al. 2023 to all locust upsurges when the mechanism is specific to the 2019-2020 event and may not apply to upsurges originating from other breeding areas.
kill_condition: A reviewer identifies a public dataset providing weekly or more frequent locust presence/absence data at district-level grain for at least three East African countries (Ethiopia, Kenya, Somalia) with less than 2-week reporting lag and consistent methodology, or finds that FAO has made eLocust3 data publicly accessible for independent validation, or finds that a peer-reviewed study has validated a remote sensing-based early warning signal with a false-alarm rate below 30 percent at a lead time of at least 3 weeks across multiple upsurge events.
limitations: The claim is about public data availability for locust early warning analysis, not about actual locust presence or breeding activity in specific locations.

The assessment that no public dataset provides district-level weekly locust data is an inference from reviewing FAO data portals and published literature, not a systematic survey of all national locust control unit databases.
Some East African countries may have national locust surveillance systems with finer-grain data that is not publicly accessible; these were not reviewed.
The kill condition thresholds (30 percent false-alarm rate, 3-week lead time) are based on the operational needs implied by FAO control response timelines, not on a formal requirements analysis.
